items.q: Do higher interest rates affect all businesses equally?
items.correct: No — firms with large borrowings or selling goods bought on credit, like cars and houses, are hit hardest, while cash-rich firms selling essentials feel far less impact.
items.misconception: Interest rate rises hurt every business by the same amount.
items.random_wrong: Interest rates only apply to government departments.
items.why: Students apply economy-wide changes uniformly instead of analysing each firm's exposure to borrowing and credit-financed demand.

items.q: Is government legislation only a cost burden for business?
items.correct: No — laws raise compliance costs, but they also create a level playing field, build consumer trust, and can open opportunities for firms that adapt quickly.
items.misconception: All government regulation simply makes businesses worse off.
items.random_wrong: Legislation only affects businesses with over 1,000 employees.
items.why: Students give one-sided answers; balanced evaluation of legal influences earns higher marks.

items.q: Does a weaker exchange rate harm every business in a country?
items.correct: No — exporters gain because their goods become cheaper abroad, while importers face higher costs; the effect depends on where a firm buys and sells.
items.misconception: A falling currency is always bad news for businesses.
items.random_wrong: Exchange rates are fixed by individual companies.
items.why: Students miss that depreciation helps exporters and hurts importers — the direction of trade matters.

items.q: During a recession, demand is likely to fall fastest for:
items.correct: Luxury products such as foreign holidays
items.why: Correct answer: Luxury products such as foreign holidays. “Staple foods such as bread” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A rise in interest rates is most likely to:
items.correct: Increase loan repayment costs for businesses with debt
items.why: It's Increase loan repayment costs for businesses with debt. “Increase consumer spending on credit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a country's currency gets stronger, its exporters typically find that:
items.correct: Their goods become more expensive for foreign buyers
items.misconception: Their goods become cheaper for foreign buyers
items.why: Correct answer: Their goods become more expensive for foreign buyers. “Their goods become cheaper for foreign buyers” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is an example of fiscal policy?
items.correct: An increase in income tax rates
items.misconception: The central bank raising interest rates
items.random_wrong: A trade union calling a strike
items.why: The answer examiners want is: An increase in income tax rates. “The central bank raising interest rates” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The exchange rate is £1 = $1.50. A UK product priced at £200 costs how many dollars abroad?
items.why: Exact answer: 300 $. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
